one_line_takeaway: LLM watermarking cannot simultaneously survive federated-learning fine-tuning noise (radioactivity) and resist an adversarial server using strong Byzantine-robust aggregation (RandEigen) to filter out watermark-bearing updates, exposing a fundamental radioactivity-robustness-utility trade-off for FL-based data provenance.
key_finding: KGW+ is strongly radioactive in FL (detectable with p as low as 2.41e-8 on C4 and 4.96e-24 on Alpaca at only 6.6% watermarked data, improving with larger model size and larger epsilon), but KTH+ is not radioactive in FL at all (p-values stay near the null value ~0.48-0.50 regardless of model size or epsilon, because its detector cannot accumulate signal across prompts). Critically, for every configuration where KGW+ was radioactive under VanillaFL, the RandEigen-based active server (ActiveFL) drove the post-fine-tuning p-value back to statistical insignificance (e.g., 2.41e-8 -> 0.613 on C4/410M; 4.96e-24 -> 0.282 on Alpaca/410M), and this held across all tested epsilon values (6.6%-30.0%) even though robustness (ER) improved somewhat with larger epsilon (3.5% -> 9.9% ER as epsilon rises 6.6% -> 30%), the watermark remained undetectable (p >= 0.01) in every case. Filtering effectiveness depended on distributional shift: when clean clients also trained on synthetic (LLM-generated) data rather than natural text, the generative-model-induced shift disappeared and RandEigen could no longer separate watermark from clean updates (ER jumps to 60.2-60.7%), but in that regime the watermark was also not radioactive to begin with. Larger watermark logit bias delta improves radioactivity but simultaneously makes the watermark easier for the aggregator to filter (ER drops from 60.2% to 0.7% as delta rises 0->5), so no delta value achieves both properties. Increasing epsilon to improve radioactivity/robustness also degrades global model utility (entropy loss rises from 3.156 to 3.161 as epsilon goes 0%->30%). The paper's overall conclusion: no evaluated combination of watermark scheme/hyperparameters achieves radioactivity, robustness to an active aggregator, and acceptable utility simultaneously.
core_contribution: The paper introduces 'federated data provenance' -- using LLM watermarking of client-generated synthetic training data to attribute contributions in federated learning (FL) fine-tuning -- and shows this approach is fundamentally broken against an active server. It (1) empirically demonstrates that hashing-based LLM watermarks (KGW+) remain radioactive (statistically detectable) after FedOpt-style FL fine-tuning even when only 6.6% of a client's data is watermarked, with p-values down to ~2.4e-8 (C4) and ~5.0e-24 (Alpaca); (2) formalizes a new threat model, ActiveFL, in which the FL server itself is an active adversary that swaps simple averaging for a strong Byzantine-robust aggregator (RandEigen, from Lee et al. 2025) to filter out watermark-carrying client updates as statistical outliers while preserving global model utility; and (3) shows empirically that under this active server, none of the evaluated radioactive watermark configurations remain detectable (post-aggregation p-values collapse to ~0.2-0.6, i.e., indistinguishable from the null), even as the watermark fraction epsilon is pushed up to 30%. The paper's own claim is a fundamental three-way trade-off among watermark radioactivity, robustness (to an adversarial aggregator), and model utility -- not merely a reproduction of prior radioactivity results.
threat_model: Two settings are defined. VanillaFL: an honest-but-non-participating server that simply FedOpt-averages client updates; used as a baseline to measure whether watermark signal survives FL noise/client drift at all. ActiveFL: the server is the adversary. It follows the rest of the FL protocol (correct client selection, communication rounds, no tampering with client training) but is free to choose its aggregation function AGG, replacing averaging with a strong Byzantine-robust aggregator (FIL, instantiated as RandEigen) whose goal is twofold and formalized in the paper's Definition 2 (FL Robustness): (1) keep the resulting model's utility close (under metric E, e.g. entropy loss/BLEU/ROUGE) to what honest averaging over clean updates would produce, and (2) drive the watermark detection test's p-value on the resulting model above the significance threshold, i.e., evade provenance detection. Assumptions: no privacy/inference attacks are considered (clients' raw data stays private from the server and other clients); all clients are honest and follow the protocol (only the server is adversarial); watermarking clients share a secret key and generative model unknown to the server; the server does not control what fraction of clients watermark and has no separate clean reference dataset. This is a provenance/attribution-evasion threat model specific to FL aggregation, not a jailbreak, extraction, or poisoning threat model in the usual sense.
limitations: Evaluated only two watermarking families (KGW+, KTH+) and one robust aggregator (RandEigen), though the paper notes Aaronson & Kirchner's Gumbel watermark was also tested in an appendix and found non-robust. Model scale is capped at 410M parameters (small by current LLM standards); it is unclear whether radioactivity/robustness trends extrapolate to billion-parameter models. The FL setup is fixed at N=30 clients with a specific FedOpt configuration; results may not generalize to other aggregation topologies, client counts, or non-IID data distributions beyond what was tested. The threat model assumes an honest client population and a single adversarial party (the server); it does not consider colluding malicious clients, privacy-inference attacks, or a server that also tampers with model architecture/training beyond choosing AGG. The robust aggregator's theoretical guarantees (RandEigen eliminating outliers) only formally hold for epsilon < 8.3%, yet empirically the watermark stayed undetectable even at epsilon=30%, which the paper flags but does not fully theoretically explain. No code is released with the paper (promised only after acceptance), limiting immediate independent verification.

datasets_used: Alpaca (instruction-following QA pairs, Taori et al.)
quantitative_results: KGW+ radioactivity (VanillaFL, C4, epsilon=6.6%): p=0.169 (70M, not significant), p=1.27e-3 (160M), p=2.41e-8 (410M)
KGW+ radioactivity (VanillaFL, Alpaca, epsilon=6.6%): p=0.013 (70M), p=1.59e-11 (160M), p=4.96e-24 (410M)
KTH+ radioactivity: p stays ~0.48-0.50 across all model sizes and datasets (not radioactive)
KGW+ robustness (epsilon=6.6%): p rises from 1.27e-3 (Vanilla) to 0.550 (Active, RandEigen) on C4/160M; 2.41e-8 -> 0.613 on C4/410M; 1.59e-11 -> 0.231 on Alpaca/160M; 4.96e-24 -> 0.282 on Alpaca/410M
Effect of epsilon on KGW+ robustness against RandEigen: ER rises 3.5% (eps=6.6%) -> 8.4% (eps=16.6%) -> 9.9% (eps=30.0%); post-Active p-value remains >=0.01 (undetectable) at all three epsilon levels

Effect of synthetic vs natural clean data on filterability: ER=60.2% (KGW+) / 60.7% (KTH+) when clean clients also use synthetic data, vs ER=0.7% (KGW+) / 0.2% (KTH+) when clean clients use natural data
Effect of logit bias delta on KGW+ robustness: ER falls from 60.2% (delta=0) to 21.6% (delta=1) to 1.0% (delta=3) to 0.7% (delta=5); OFR falls from 92.9% to 40.7% over same range
Effect of delta on radioactivity: p-value drops from ~1e-3 to ~1e-6 as delta increases 3.0->6.0 (Figure 4)
Utility cost: entropy loss increases from 3.156 to 3.161 as epsilon increases from 0% to 30.0%
baselines_compared: KGW+ (Kirchenbauer et al. 2024, hashing-based green-list watermark)
KTH+ (Kuditipudi et al. 2024, distortion-free non-hashing watermark)
RandEigen (Lee et al. 2025, quasi-linear-time strong Byzantine-robust aggregator) as the ActiveFL aggregator vs. plain averaging (VanillaFL/FedOpt, Reddi et al. 2021) as the baseline aggregator
